coredata.dc:title: Amino acid-based carbon quantum dot modified MIL-53 (Fe): Investigation of its visible-driven photocatalytic activity provided by a highly efficient photoreactor
coredata.dc:creator: Rezaei, Hooman
Zinatizadeh, Ali Akbar
Joshaghani, Mohammad
Zinadini, Sirus
coredata.dc:description: As a promising approach to addressing the ever-increasing concern of dye contaminants, visible-driven amino acid-based carbon quantum dot/MIL-53 (Fe) binary composite with excellent photocatalytic activity and stability was successfully fabricated for the first time by solvothermal technique and employed for methylene blue (MB) photodegradation in a highly efficient photoreactor. The optimum nanocomposite was obtained by doping 3 wt % amino acid-based carbon quantum dot in toMIL-53 (Fe) pores and characterized utilizing several analyses, including XRD, FTIR, FESEM, DRS, EDX, TEM, EIS and PL to investigate its morphology, optical, and electrochemical properties. Enhanced separation of charge carriers as well as increased visible light absorption capacity of MOF particles led to a maximum photocatalytic MB removal of 98%when employing the optimum nanocompositeunder visible light exposure. Central composite design (CCD) was employed to investigate the effects of four independent variables, including catalyst loading (0.5–2.5 g/L), dye concentration (20–50 ppm), initial pH (5–11), and irradiation time (0.5–2.5 h), and optimize the MB removal efficiency of the catalyst. The results verified the deteriorating impact of increasing the dye concentration as well as decreasing the catalyst dosage, initial pH, and irradiation time on the photodegradation efficiency of the hybrid nanocomposite. The reusability results further confirmed the stability and reliability of the nanocomposite, indicating that it could be used in subsequent runs with relatively stable photocatalytic activity. Photocurrent response experiments further confirmed that the amino acid-based carbon quantum dots enhanced the separation and migration of photoinduced electrons and holes. Active species trapping studies suggested that holes (h+) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were more prominent than superoxide radicals (•O2−) throughout the organic degradation process. Ultimately, a potential photocatalytic mechanism was proposed in light of the test results.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a promising approach to addressing the ever-increasing concern of dye contaminants, visible-driven amino acid-based carbon quantum dot/MIL-53 (Fe) binary composite with excellent photocatalytic activity and stability was successfully fabricated for the first time by solvothermal technique and employed for methylene blue (MB) photodegradation in a highly efficient photoreactor. The optimum nanocomposite was obtained by doping 3 wt % amino acid-based carbon quantum dot in toMIL-53 (Fe) pores and characterized utilizing several analyses, including XRD, FTIR, FESEM, DRS, EDX, TEM, EIS and PL to investigate its morphology, optical, and electrochemical properties. Enhanced separation of charge carriers as well as increased visible light absorption capacity of MOF particles led to a maximum photocatalytic MB removal of 98%when employing the optimum nanocompositeunder visible light exposure. Central composite design (CCD) was employed to investigate the effects of four independent variables, including catalyst loading (0.5–2.5 g/L), dye concentration (20–50 ppm), initial pH (5–11), and irradiation time (0.5–2.5 h), and optimize the MB removal efficiency of the catalyst. The results verified the deteriorating impact of increasing the dye concentration as well as decreasing the catalyst dosage, initial pH, and irradiation time on the photodegradation efficiency of the hybrid nanocomposite. The reusability results further confirmed the stability and reliability of the nanocomposite, indicating that it could be used in subsequent runs with relatively stable photocatalytic activity. Photocurrent response experiments further confirmed that the amino acid-based carbon quantum dots enhanced the separation and migration of photoinduced electrons and holes. Active species trapping studies suggested that holes (h+) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were more prominent than superoxide radicals (•O2−) throughout the organic degradation process. Ultimately, a potential photocatalytic mechanism was proposed in light of the test results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water resources depletion and environmental pollution have become the two major concerns for human beings [1,2]. In recent years, the abundance and sustainability of solar energy have shifted global interest to the use of photocatalytic process in environmental remediation and energy production [3–5]. Photocatalysis has been widely used to as an approach to curb the challenge of water contamination due to its complete degradation of persistent organic compounds under ambient conditions [6–8]. Nevertheless, possessing drawbacks such as wide band gap, frequent agglomeration in solution, rapid recombination rate of e−/h+ pairs, and only responding to UV irradiation led to low photocatalytic capacity and photoenergy consumption efficiency in single-phase photocatalysts [9–11]. Integrating diverse functional groups to fabricate a composite photocatalyst is an efficient way to address these drawbacks [12–14]. As a result, a variety of semiconductor nanocomposites have emerged from the combination of semiconductor nanoparticles with other semiconductor materials [15–17], metals [18], and conjugated polymers [19], using different synthetic methods. These multicomponent and binary composites prepared using the aforementioned techniques might outperform single-phase photocatalysts in terms of photocatalytic performance.

In the last decade, scientists have been lured to the easily tunable porous structures of various metal organic frameworks (MOFs) as novel emerging crystalline materials due to their rich topology and ultra-high specific surface areas [11,20]. In photocatalysis, the unique structure of MOF makes it an exceptional material to not only act as an absorbent of light, prominent for improving the performance of photocatalytic activity, but also combine with other functional materials. Meanwhile, carbon compounds such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene have also been intensively researched in recent years due to their remarkable optical and electrical characteristics [12,21,22]. Owing to their unique physicochemical properties including low toxicity, efficient light harvesting, and tunable photoluminescence [23], CQDs have been applied in a lot of front-end investigation fields, such as photocatalytic processes and photo-electrochemical reactions [24], chemical luminescence interactions [25], and fluorescent detections [26]. CQDs have a strong ability to capture light, allowing them to successfully boost photogenerated electron transmission while decreasing the recombination rate of e−/h+ pairs. In other words, when applied as a co-catalyst, CQD scan act as a photosensitizer, spectral converter, or an electron receptor in various photocatalytic systems [27,28].

Despite their many advantages, neither MOFs nor CQDs can serve as a full photocatalyst on their own due to limitations such as instability and low light absorption of MOFs, as well as the rapid recombination rate of photogenerated charges and simple aggregation of CQDs [29–31]. Nevertheless, MOFs with well-ordered porous structures provide a large surface area for CQDs, effectively assisting them in photoelectric performance and preventing agglomeration. Furthermore, due to the matched band gap and close interface between MOFs and CQDs, their integration could increase the rate of the photogenerated charge separation, successfully prevent the recombination of e−/h+ pairs, and hence improve the photocatalytic efficiency [32,33].

Holding the key to the efficiency and accuracy of the photocatalytic experiments, the characteristic photoreactor design and fabrication is quite challenging in terms of providing a platform to study the operational parameters of the photocatalytic process from varying perspectives (e.g. Varying the type and dosage of catalyst, type and concentration of pollutants, light intensity, etc.) [34,35]. Consequently, the reactor constructed in this study, allowing for the simultaneous and separate use of different types of radiation sources with different optical properties, simultaneous use of several cells with varying volumes, and simultaneous use of two methods of magnetic mixing and aeration (with a variety of gases such as N2, O2, O3, air), serves two major objectives:
1
By boosting the reliability and precision of analysis conditions, using multiple cells in a reactor for performance and comparison studies enhances data quality and saves electrical energy consumption.
2
The process of evaluating and optimizing the volumetric yield of the reactor will be facilitated by altering the volume of cells employed in the reactor.

Moreover, several intriguing studies focused on CQD-embedded MOFs for optical applications [36], have encouraged us to look into amino acid-based CQD-embedded MOF composite as a potential photocatalytic hybrid for dye degradation. While providing a facile method for synthesizing amino acid-based CQDs, this study investigates the novel amino acid-based CQD/MIL-53 (Fe) nanocomposite with significantly improved dye photodegradation. The photocatalytic performance of the nanocomposite was further evaluated, and the effect of processing parameters such as catalyst loading, dye concentration, initial pH, and irradiation time was investigated using response surface methodology (RSM) in the aforementioned innovative photoreactor. The photocatalytic mechanism was also proposed, shedding light on several aspects of amino acid-based CQD's introduction into the MOF-based nanocomposite, leading to its photocatalytic performance enhancement.

Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O), l-histidine amino acid, ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), 1, 4-benzenediol-carboxylic acid (1, 4-BDC), TEMPOL (4-Hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl), potassium iodide, tert-butanol (t-BuOH) were provided from Merck Co., Germany. All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

MIL-53 (Fe) as the bare photocatalyst was synthesized by a one-step solvothermal technique [37]. In a typical procedure, 1, 4-BDC and FeCl3.6H2O were dissolved in DMF solution with a molar ratio of 1:1:280, respectively. After 1 h of stirring accompanied by a 20-min ultrasonic treatment at 25O C, the prepared solution was placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 150O C for overnight. After being cooled to room temperature, the light orange powder was separated form reaction solution by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min and washed four times with DMF, ethanol and water, respectively. Then, the purified solid was dried at 100 °C for 24 h under vacuum condition [38]. Fig. 1 a illustrates a schematic diagram of the MIL-53 (Fe) synthesis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino acid-based CQD-embedded MIL-53 (Fe) nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the activation of as-prepared MIL-53(Fe) particles was carried out by dispersing them in methanol under 30 min ultra-sonication followed by magnetic stirring for 24h at room temperature. The MIL-53 (Fe) particles were then centrifuged, washed twice with fresh methanol, and dried overnight at 80 °C under vacuum conditions. In order to provide the C and N sources, the activated MIL-53 (Fe) particles were added to certain quantities (1–5%) of l-histidine which had been dissolved in 50 mL deionized water. The prepared solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and the temperature was adjustedto190 °C for 12 h to synthesize the modifying agent referred to as N-CQD and then embed it in to MIL-53 (Fe) structure. Following that, the samples were centrifuged several times, rinsed with methanol and purified water, then dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The product hereafter will be referred to as NCM (Fe) henceforth. The prepared nanocomposites were designated X-NCM (Fe), where X represents the quantity of N-CQDs, with X = 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5% [39].The synthesis of N-CQDs and X-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite is depicted schematically in Fig. 1b and c.

The crystal structure of the as-prepared catalyst was studied using X-ray Diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/MAX-TTRIII (CBO)).The element composition and microscopic morphology of the samples could be determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai) and field emission scanning microscope (FESEM) analysis equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (FESEM/EDX, Hitachi, 4300 SE/N). An FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS10) was used to record FT-IR spectra of powder samples. An organic carbon analyzer (Analytik Jena multi-N/C 3100) was used to quantify total organic carbon (TOC). Electrochemical measurements were analyzed using potentiostat/galvanostatic/impedance analyzer PalmSens3.

The porous structure and specific surface area of the catalyst was investigated by employing nitrogen isotherm analysis (Belsorp-max-Microtrac).UV–Vis Diffuse Reflectance Spectrophotometer (DRS, Hitachi U–3900H) as well as UV–visible spectroscopy (Unico Dual beam:4800) were employed to assess the sample's optical properties. By detecting the fluorescence emitted by the samples, photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL, Varian Cary) was also used to assess the separation efficiencies of the photogenerated charges of the photocatalysts.

The body of this photoreactor is made of aluminum with a diameter of 1 mm sheet in a rectangular shape with dimensions of 25 × 25 × 50 cm, and cylindrical cells with varying capacities of 10, 20, 50, 100, and 1000 cc are employed in its central space with the objective of maximizing the surface under radiation. In this investigation, 20 cc cells were used, and the holder for the 20 cc cell has 12 positions, 8 of which are exposed to direct light. Inside each cell, mass transfer can be accomplished through two methods: mixing with a magnetic stirrer and an aeration system. Four 10 × 20 cm vents are installed side by side on the reactor's four rectangular sides, with the opposite ones housing the light stands and the cooling system's jet fans, respectively.

The power supply for this photoreactor is a direct voltage system that can be adjusted between 12 and 35000 V, providing the required electrical energy to activate the radiation barrier, mixing system, and cooling. Fig. 1S depicts real photos of this setup, which are numbered at various components, including the reactor's main body.

According to the schematic design of the reactor shown in Fig. 2S, the cooling system of this reactor employs 7 high-power jet fans to control the air temperature within the reactor, with 3 of these fans fixed on the main body of the reactor and 4 on the moveable light stands.

The type of radiation, its intensity, and temperature may all be altered by altering the type of lamp and its radiant power. The optical specifications of the 300 W xenon lamp were recorded and validated using a competent light meter (China model UPRtek PG100 N), the results of which are shown in Fig. 3S. The optical spectrum and radiative properties of the xenon lamp employed in this investigation, as shown in Fig. 3S, are functionally compatible with the radiative characteristics of sunlight [40].

In order to investigate the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized nanocomposites, the xenon radiation supply of the photoreactor was used as the sunlight simulator lamp (with a radiant power of 300 W and a cut-off filter of ʎ< 420 nm) for the photodegradation of dye. As the dye contaminant, methylene blue (MB) with a maximum absorption wavelength of 664 nm was used. Initially, eight 30-ml tubes were mounted inside the photoreactor. Then, 30 mL of MB solution with specified concentrations and an initial pH adjusted in line with the process design parameters was poured into each reactor cell. Following that, particular amounts of nanocomposite were weighed and added within each cell depending on the values provided in the process design. To obtain dye adsorption equilibrium on the catalyst surface, the solutions were agitated for 45 min in each cell using a separate stirrer under the dark conditions provided within the reactor. To track the dye content in each cell, a specific amount of solution was taken every 15 min, centrifuged for 5 min to separate the catalyst from the solution, and the remaining concentration of the contaminant was detected with a spectrophotometer. The following equation (Eq. (1)) was used to quantify the photocatalytic removal percentage of nanocomposites:
(1)
R e m o v a l

∁ o − ∁ t

× 100
where C0 and Ct are the initial and time-varying concentrations of MB dye, respectively [41]. Various scavengers, including TEMPOL as a superoxide radical scavenger and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) as a hydroxyl radical scavenger, were employed in the trapping experiment to explore the process of photocatalytic degradation. For the trapping experiment, the scavenger doses were set at 1 mmol.

Platinum wire served as the auxiliary electrode in the electrochemical cell, which also contained an ITO-coated photocatalyst as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and 0.5 M of Na2SO4 as the electrolyte. The samples were thoroughly rinsed with deionized water and the electrolyte before being subjected to the Mott-Schottky (M.S.) measurements. Before starting the tests, the samples and contacts were given the opportunity to soak for 30 min in the electrolytic solution. The aforementioned three-electrode arrangement was used to conduct the photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. A 300-W cut-off filter-equipped Xenon bulb (λ < 420 nm) served as the light source [42].

One of the most commonly used statistical approaches in experimental design for assessing relationships, modeling, and optimizing independent experimental variables is central composite design (CCD) in conjunction with response surface methodology (RSM) [41]. In the present study, a three-level CCD with four independent variables was designed for modeling MB photocatalytic degradation in the NCM (Fe) system. As shown in Table 1 , the experimental variables, including catalyst loading (0.5–2.5 g/L), MB concentration (20–50 ppm), initial pH (5–11), and the irradiation time (0.5–2.5 h) and their respective levels were selected based on the literature.

According to Eq. (2), CCD at 3 levels (α = −1, 0, 1) consists of 2nfactorial runs, 2n axial runs, and nc central runs:
(2)
N = 2k-q + 2 × k + n
c
where k, q, and nc represent the number of factors, a fraction of the number factor, and the central point's replicate number, respectively. As a result, a total of 30 runs were designed for each dye removal based on CCD. To assess the validity of the fitted models, the contribution of processing variables to the response, and their interactions, the analysis of variance (ANOVA) has been used.

In order to determine the optimum content of N-CQD (1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 wt%) as a C–N doping agent, after 45 min of adsorption in the dark, the photocatalytic degradation of MB was conducted under visible light irradiation. A scan be seen in Fig. 2 , after 120 min irradiation at [MB] = 35 ppm, pH = 9, while using 1.5 g/L of catalyst, the MB degradation efficiency as a function of weight fraction of N-CQD was plotted. The MB removal efficiency of bare MIL-53 (Fe) was only 69% after 2 h of visible irradiation. The results demonstrated the positive impact of increasing N-CQD loading from 1 to 3 wt%, with MB photocatalytic degradation rising from 69% to 98%. However, further increasing the dose of nonmetal dopant (from 3 to 5 wt %) led to a decrease in MB removal (86%).

As aforementioned, the nanohybrid with 3% N-CQD loading exhibited the best photocatalytic activity under simulated sunlight illumination. This finding might be attributed to the 3-NCM (Fe) sample's efficient charge carrier separation, narrow band gap, and low recombination rate of photogenerated e−/h + pairs as compared to the MIL-53 (Fe) and other nanocomposites. Furthermore, the detrimental impact of increased N-CQD loading in nanohybrids (>3 wt%) on photocatalytic degradation may be ascribed to excess N-CQDs covering the active sites of MIL-53 (Fe) or to the accelerated rate of photogenerated e−/h+ recombination.

Under similar conditions, as depicted in Fig. 3 a, the 3-NCM (Fe) nanohybrid removed the majority of MB molecules after 120 min, whereas MIL-53 (Fe) particles led to a MB removal efficiency of only 69%, suggesting the superiority of the 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite in visible-driven MB photodegradation. Meanwhile, it was further proved that the amount of N-CQDs loaded into NCM (Fe) nanocomposites may be used to tailor their visible-driven activities. This behavior is consistent with similar photocatalyst/dopant systems, in which overloaded dopants serve as recombination centers while suppressing incident light for the main photocatalysts.

Also, photocatalytic degradation generally follows the first-order kinetics equation. And Fig. 3b displays the plots –Ln (Ct/C0) versus t (h). The equation is as follows:
(3) –Ln (Ct/C0) = KT
where C0 initial concentration of MB (mg/L) on obtained materials at adsorption equilibrium, and Ct represents the concentrations of MB on obtained materials at time t (h). K represents the reaction rate constant for first-order kinetics. Fig. 3b clearly indicates that the photocatalytic degradation curve is well-fit to a pseudo-first-order correlation [43].

In comparison to other prepared materials, 3-NCM (Fe) has a substantially higher k value, as indicated in Table 2 . These results imply that 3-NCM (Fe) can serve as photocatalysts for the removal of MB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the prepared nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of the synthesized nanomaterials in the region of 450-4000 cm−1 were used to investigate the functional groups of the MIL-53 (Fe) and 3-NCM (Fe), revealing several significant peaks (Fig. 4 a). The FTIR spectrum of 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite displays characteristic absorption peaks of MIL-53 (Fe) and N-CQDs. The prominent bands in the MIL-53 (Fe) FTIR spectrum are attributed to C=O stretching (1646 cm−1), OH vibration (1435 cm−1), C–O stretching (1381 cm−1), C–H vibrations (738 cm−1), aromatic ring stretching (714 cm−1), and Fe–O stretching vibrations (485 cm−1). Furthermore, the absorption peaks at 1248 and 864 cm−1 for the C–O stretching modes, along with the C=O stretching vibration peaks obtained at 1709 cm−1, indicate the presence of surface groups such as –OH, –COOH, and –C=O– on carbon quantum dots [44]. O–H and N–H bond vibrations might be responsible for the broad absorption bands between 3600 and 3000 cm−1. The distinctive peak, corresponding to the C–N stretching vibration and located at 1439 cm−1, shows that –NH2 was successfully incorporated into N-CQD and nanocomposite materials [39]. N-CQD/MIL-53 was successfully incorporated, as demonstrated by the FT-IR spectra (Fe). Notably, the vibration peak in the spectra of N-CQD and MIL-53 (Fe) is situated at 3168 cm−1, which is ascribed to the formation of the –C(O)–NH– bond for the reaction between the amino groups in N-CQD and the carboxyl groups in MIL-53 (Fe) [45].

The XRD patterns of the MIL-53 (Fe) and 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite are demonstrated in Fig. 4b. The characteristic peaks of MIL-53 (Fe) are in good agreement with those of earlier studies, thus demonstrating the efficient preparation of MIL-53 (Fe). The XRD pattern of MIL-53 (Fe) is characterized by multiple peaks corresponding to 9.4°, 12.7°, 17.6°, and 25.4° [46].

According to the XRD pattern of 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite, the basic structure of MIL-53 (Fe) was not damaged by the surface impregnation process used to load N-CQDs. Nonetheless, due to the low loading content of N-CQDs in MIL-53 (Fe), no significant peak for N-CQDs at about 26° was detected [44].

The size as well as the surface morphology of the prepared nanoparticles was studied by FESEM and TEM techniques. The morphologies of MIL-53 (Fe) particles before and after N-CQD formation, as shown in Fig. 5 a and b indicate that the suggested synthetic method yielded MIL-53 (Fe) particles with consistent sizes and regular geometries. The MIL-53 (Fe) particles had a hexagonal bipyramid form, as shown in the magnified SEM image (Fig. 5c), with an average length of around 480 nm and a width of about 320 nm [47]. Fig. 5g and h, illustrate TEM images of a single MIL-53(Fe) particle, further confirming its consistent shape and size. As depicted in Fig. 5d and e, following the in-situ formation of N-CQDs within this MIL-53 (Fe) particle, the resultant 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposites had the same morphology as MIL-53 (Fe) particles. According to the results obtained, the formation of N-CQDs under low-temperature carbonization conditions has little to no impact on the shape and structure of MIL-53 (Fe) particles. Due to the formation of multiple N-CQDs following the carbonization step, the NCM (Fe) particles appear rough and black under TEM inspection (Fig. 5i and j).

Fig. 4S depicts the EDX spectra and EDS elemental mapping of the bare photocatalyst as well as its nanocomposite, indicating that both are primarily comprised of C, O, and Fe. The presence of small amounts of N element in the EDX spectrum of synthesized nanocomposite can further support the successful N-CQD formation in the structure of nanocomposite. Moreover, elemental mapping of the 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite using EDS can confirm the presence of Fe, C, O, and N in a homogeneous spatial distribution (Fig. 4S). DRS, Tauc plots, and PL analyses were used to investigate the optical characteristics of synthesized photocatalysts, as shown in Fig. 6a and b and 5 S, respectively. The introduction of C and N energy levels into the MIL-Fe (53) frame via nonmetal doping agents (N-CQD derived from l-histidine source) resulted in new electronic impurity levels between the VB and CB of this photocatalyst, while decreasing the band gap energy (Fig. 6a).

According to the Tauc plots in Fig. 6b, the band gap energies of bare MIL-53 (Fe), 1-NCM (Fe), 3-NCM (Fe), 5-NCM (Fe) nanocomposites were determined to be 2.76, 2.69, 2.63 and 2.80 eV, respectively [48]. The optical response of the N-CQD/MIL-53 (Fe) nanocomposite was typically boosted as the quantity of N-CQDs introduced increased. Due to the potentially detrimental impact of >3 wt% N-CQD loadings on the rate of light absorption, the sample with the highest concentration of N-CQDs (5 wt%) exhibited lower light absorption than the 3-NCM (Fe) sample, which is in complete agreement with the activity performance results, as previously shown in Fig. 2.

The PL analysis was also carried out at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 5S. In comparison to 1-NCM (Fe), 3-NCM (Fe), and 5-NCM (Fe) nanocomposites, which have maximum near band edge emission (NBE) at 446 nm, 447 nm, and 448 nm, respectively, pure MIL-53 (Fe) seems to have a lower maximum NBE. Thus, the development of the composites has resulted in a minor red-shift. Intriguingly, the emission intensity has dramatically lowered as a result of the heterostructure formation of N-CQD with MIL-53 (Fe), suggesting the presence of an effective electronic interaction between MIL-53 (Fe) and N-CQD. A lower rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs recombining in the heterostructures is suggested by the declining PL intensity. Effective charge transfer between MIL-53 (Fe) and N-CQD across the interface area is facilitated by the formation of heterostructure with N-CQD. It is indeed worth noting that 3-NCM (Fe) composite shows the lowest PL intensity. However, since excess N-CQDs on 5-NCM (Fe) serve as recombination sites for the charge carriers, a further rise in N-CQD concentration elevates the PL intensity [49]. Such PL quenching implies that 3-NCM (Fe) composites guarantee the most effective charge transfer required for efficient photocatalytic activity. Moreover, a modest modification in the composites' PL emission profile relative to that of bare MIL-53 (Fe) also supports the existence of N-CQD in the heterostructure. This is a consequence of the interactions between MIL-53 (Fe) and N-CQD emissions [39]. As can be seen, the introduction of N-CQD nanoparticles considerably reduces the intensity of the photocatalyst's distinctive fluorescence peak, suggesting their efficient contribution to charge separation and transfer, thus boosting photocatalytic activity [50].

As illustrated in Fig. 6S and Table 3 , adsorption of N2 rapidly increased at lower relative pressures and adsorption saturation occurred when particular relative pressure was reached. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas of MIL-53 (Fe) and 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposites were 22.2 and 16.8 m2/g, respectively [51]. The pore size distributions are shown in Fig. 7S, and we have proven through tests that the surface pore sizes of the MIL-53 (Fe) and 3-NCM (Fe) materials are 1.85 nm and 1.96 nm, respectively, proving the microporosity of the materials [51]. The decreased BET surface area of the MOF particles following the addition of N-CQDs implies that numerous N-CQD particles have occupied the pore vacancies in the MOF particles.

To match the CCD experimental data, the response surface model was developed using analysis of variance (ANOVA), and the results are given in Table 4 . The reduced quadratic model with a p value less than 0.0001 and an F value of 174.12 was investigated. Due to their p-values of <0.05, the model terms A (catalyst loading), B (dye concentration), C (initial pH), D (irradiation duration), A2, BD, and D2were all significant and had an imperative impact on the response (MB removal efficiency). Furthermore, the lack of fit of 0.671 verified the insignificant difference between predicted and experienced data [52].The reduced quadratic model for MB removal suggested is expressed as follows:
(4) MB removal (%) = +88.66 + 13.94A–5.78B + 7.94C + 10.01D + 2.44 BD - 33.44 A2 -10.94 D2

According to Eq. (4), increasing the dye concentration seems to have a negative impact on the response, but increasing the catalyst loading, initial pH of the solution and irradiation period has the opposite effect. The order of each parameter was determined by its respective F value given in Table 4 as well as its coefficient in Eq. (4) as follows [53]: A2> A > D2> D > C > B > BD.

A2 and BD had the greatest and least impact on the response, respectively, among the factors considered. The experimental data is compatible with the proposed model's results, as indicated by the high coefficient of determination (R2) value and an adjusted R2 close to 1 (Table 4). The adequate precision, which evaluates the signal to noise ratio, was 44.35, indicating adequate signals [54].

As shown in Fig. 8S–a, the experimental results perfectly matched the predicted model data. The model's adequacy was further verified by the residuals' normal probability graph, as shown in Fig. 8S–b. Moreover, Fig. 8S–c depicts externally studentized residuals versus predicted values, confirming the model's high adequacy [55].

Fig. 7 provides the 3D surface and contour plots of A (catalyst loading) and B (dye concentration) variables at an adjusted initial pH of 9 during 1.5 h irradiation, highlighting their impact on MB removal efficiency. As shown in Fig. 7a and b, increasing the MB concentration from 20 to 50 led to decreasing dye removal efficiency from 98 to 84 in percent regardless of time. While the initial dye concentration increases, dye adsorption on the catalyst surface occurs, resulting in saturation of the photocatalyst's active sites and poisoning by excess organic molecules and intermediates. This phenomenon may have an inhibitory impact on light penetration as well as the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), thus restricting the photodegradation efficiency [56]. In addition, as shown in Fig. 7, the response increased in proportion to photocatalyst loading up to 1.5 g/L due to an increase in the number of available active sites on the surface of the catalyst for the adsorption of dye molecules. However, as the photocatalyst concentration rose from 1.5 to 2.5 g/L, the turbidity of the reaction solution increases too (from 5 to 10 NTU) that led to the decrease in both light penetration and absorption of efficient photons, resulting in a slight decrease in the response. Furthermore, at >1.5 g/L photocatalyst concentrations, nanoparticle sedimentation and scattering might have occurred, decreasing the photoactivated volume of the suspension [57].

The MB photodegradation was optimized by applying RSM-CCD to maximize the degradation efficiency of dye in 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite. Based on results obtained, the optimum processing variables for MB removal were found to be 1.5 g/L catalyst concentration, initial pH of 9, 35 mg/L MB concentration, and 120 min irradiation time.

The specific removal rate (SRR) as well as the photodegradation efficiency of MB were also compared to investigate the treatment capacity of both 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite and bare MIL-53 (Fe) as shown in Fig. 10 a. According to the plot obtained, the maximum SRR values for 3-NCM (Fe) and MIL-53 (Fe) were 11.43 and 5.75 of pollutant removed per g of the catalyst in 1h, respectively, confirming the superiority of the 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite in photodegradation of MB.

Furthermore, the mineralization of MB and its photocatalytic degradation to carbon dioxide, water, and mineral ions were investigated by measuring the solution's TOC value [59]. Under optimal operating conditions (initial dye concentration = 35 mg/L, reaction time = 120 min, catalyst loading = 1.5 g/L and min, and initial pH = 9), Fig. 10b showed the decolorization and TOC removal efficiency of the photocatalytic process consisting of MIL-53 (Fe) and 3-NCM (Fe). When compared to color removal efficiency, Fig. 10b exhibits a reasonable drop in TOC. Further evidence supports the conclusion that the removal efficiency values of MIL-53 (Fe) with 3-NCM (Fe) after 120 min TOC reaction were 58 and 90%, respectively. One can conclude that the performance of 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite was remarkable, not only in the process of decolorization but also in the mineralization of the dye.

Playing a key role in obtaining cost-effectiveness and wide-spread industrial application, the reusability of the as-prepared photocatalysts was studied by the photocatalytic degradation of MB after 5 cycles at optimum conditions (Fig. 11 a). Throughout the cycles 1 to 5, the nanocomposites may be separated and recovered with a loss of 1–10%. According to the results obtained, the reused 3-NCM (Fe) hybrid maintained its high photodegradation activity even after five cycles, with only about 10% of MB removal efficiency decreased.

Fig. 11b depicts the UV–Vis spectra of MB solution in the presence of 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposites when exposed to visible light. The photocatalytic performance of 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposites was evaluated by monitoring the decolorization of MB solutions through the UV–vis absorption spectra of (λmax = 665 nm). As the irradiation period lengthened, the intensity of the major characteristic peaks at 612 and 665 nm, caused by the chromophore of MB molecules, decreased [60].

The photocatalyst was recovered by centrifugation and analyzed by XRD and FTIR following its use for four cycles. All the peaks of the as-produced 3-NCM (Fe) remained clearly visible in Fig. 12 following the photocatalytic process. The 3-NCM (Fe) XRD patterns prior to and following photocatalytic reactions have been shown in Fig. 12a. However, the 3-NCM (Fe) diffraction peaks lost some of their intensity. A small variation in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 12b) indicates that the 3-NCM (Fe) composite has strong chemical and photostability stability, meaning that the structure of the photocatalyst undergoes a minor change during the photocatalytic degradation process. As a result, following repeated usage of the catalyst, the photodegradation activity gradually declines.

To further unveil the photodegradation mechanism for MB removal, we performed the photocurrent responses, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and free radical and holes trapping experiments. Photocurrent responses measurements of obtained samples were operated to reflect the separation efficiencies and migration behaviors of photo-inducted carriers [61]. To further assess the recombination of the photogenerated charge carrier of prepared MIL-53 (Fe) and 3-NCM (Fe), photocurrent measurement was conducted under intermittent light irradiation at 60-s intervals. According to Fig. 13 a, under visible light irradiation, the current density increases with the addition of N-CQDs to MIL-53 (Fe). In other words, compared to pure MIL-53 (Fe), the 3-NCM (Fe) composite displays a greater current density. The root driver of the observation is that N-CQDs regulate charges on the surface of MIL-53 (Fe) while simultaneously facilitating electron transfer [33]. Obtaining reliable information on the electrical conductivity of a material's surface is heavily reliant on the EIS. The Nyquist plots of all samples are given in Fig. 13b and c, from which it can be observed that the arc radius of these samples is in accordance with 3-NCM (Fe) < MIL-53 (Fe). Generally, a smaller arc radius indicates a more effective charge transfer for the studied sample [33,62]. Indisputable evidence that the 3-NCM (Fe) has the best efficiency for charge separation resides in the fact that it displays the minimum electrochemical impedance. Overall, this might be an indication that 3-NCM (Fe) possesses significant photocatalytic properties.

The trapping experiment was carried out to investigate the primary reactive oxygen species in the 3-NCM (Fe) photocatalytic system (Fig. 14 ). Superoxide radicals (•O2−), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and holes (h+) are often the active species generated during photocatalysis [63]. Through the use of TEMPOL as a •O2− scavenger, disodium potassium iodide (KI) as a h+ scavenger, and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) as a •OH scavenger, the primary reactive groups were further identified [64]. The results of MB photodegradation studies with various sacrificial agents and 3-NCM (Fe) photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 14. The systems containing t-BuOH and KI were clearly influenced, indicating that the major active groups involved in the reaction were ˙ OH and h+. Meanwhile, it was discovered that BQ, indicating ∙O2− species, had a negligible impact on 3-NCM's capability to remove MB efficiently [65]. A viable mechanism for the 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite in photocatalytic processes is provided in light of the aforementioned observations and assessment, as shown in Fig. 14. The light usage rate of MIL-53 (Fe) was initially markedly boosted by the N-CQDs composition. Additionally, N-CQDs have been used as electron acceptors to modify the e-transfer, which was capable of inhibiting the recombination of carriers and enhance the original material's capacity for redox reactions. After photoexcitation, a minor fraction of the h+ proceeded to the CB and recombined with e−, whereas the majority of the e− was taken up by the N-CQDs, thus generating OH. In the meantime, MB on VB was photo-degraded by h+. As a result, MB deterioration could be accomplished.

Relying on the aforementioned experimental results and related analysis, Fig. 15 depicts the photocatalytic mechanism proposed for 3-NCM (Fe). Photocatalytic MIL-53 (Fe) particles (metal-oxo clusters) are activated by the short-wavelength visible light and create photogenerated e−/h+ pairs. While being formed in-situ within the MOF particles, the embedded N-CQDs may add significant number of tiny heterojunction structures between the metal-oxo clusters and N-CQDs. Such heterojunctions may efficiently separate photogenerated e−/h+ pairs by using N-CQDs as localized electron acceptors for metal-oxo clusters, improving the photocatalytic activity of MOF particles. Simultaneously, N-CQDs embedded within MOF pores may enhance light absorption in the long-wavelength visible region, serving as photosensitizers for the MOF particles in the 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposite [66].

Table 5 shows a comparison of the photocatalytic activity of the amino acid-based CQD/MIL-53 (Fe) nanocomposite with other published works on MIL-53 (Fe) and its composites for the photodegradation of organic contaminations. As previously reported, MIL-53 (Fe) photocatalytic performance is limited by a swift recombination of electron/hole pairs [67]. In the meantime, many methods have been used to enhance the photocatalytic activity of MIL-53 (Fe), including coupling with another semiconductor and metal/nonmetal doping. In the present case, higher improvement in the photocatalytic activity of MIL-53 (Fe) has been achieved by doping it with amino acid-based CQDs as compared to the other reported results of MIL-53 (Fe) and its composites.

Through facile in-situ formation of amino acid based-CQD as the C and N source into the pores of MIL-53 (Fe), researchers were able to fabricate and characterize the visible-driven 3-NCM (Fe) nanocomposites, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic degradation of MB contaminant. The assessment of the photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposites under visible light was conducted using a novel, highly efficient photoreactor. The results indicate that the nonmetal doping agents embedded in MOF pores may serve not only as localized electron acceptors to effectively separate photogenerated electrons from MIL-53 (Fe) particles, but also as photosensitizers, ultimately resulting in a significant increase in photocatalytic activity. According to the statistical analysis carried out by RSM, increasing the visible light irradiation time (up to 2h), catalyst loading (up to 1.5 g/L), and pH (up to 9) resulted in higher MB removal efficiency, whereas, increasing the concentration of contaminant reversed the impact. Furthermore, the optimum nanocomposite was found to be readily recovered and reused for five cycles with no significant loss of photocatalytic activity. The kinetic constant value increased to 0.0284, which was 2.9 times greater than MIL-53 (Fe). Furthermore, the h+ and •OH active species are major players in MB degradation. Effective charge separation at the heterojunction interfaces, enhancing the photocatalytic capacity, was further confirmed by the photochemical experiment data. Overall, the development of MOF semiconductors, from our perspective, will be benefited by this research.

Hooman Rezaei: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Ali Akbar Zinatizadeh: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Mohammad Joshaghani: Writing – review & editing. Sirus Zinadini: Writing – review & editing.
